By June, only around 8,000 units hadn’t been returned, a source with direct knowledge of sales and return data told me.
But around 1,000 purchases were canceled before shipping, and more than $1 million worth of product has been returned.
The low sales figures also pale in comparison to the over $200 million that Humane has raised from notable Silicon Valley executives like OpenAI CEO Sam Altman and Salesforce CEO Marc Benioff.
Humane hoped to ship about 100,000 Pins within the first year, according to a source with knowledge of the plan and first reported by The New York Times.
Cuccias told The Verge that “we continue to build an incredibly talented and deeply experienced team” and are “committed to unlocking a new era of ambient and contextual computing” with the addition of Rubén Caballero, chief engineering and strategy officer, to Humane’s leadership team in June."},{"url":"https://mashable.com/article/humane-ai-pin-returns","title":"Humane AI Pins are being returned at a ridiculous pace | Mashable","content":"Humane reportedly shipped a total of around 10,000 AI Pins so far, falling far behind its expectations to sell 100,000 units in the first year.

Rabbit R1 sold 100,000 units but ...
OpenAI's Sora burned an estimated $15 million per day in compute costs while generating just $2.1 million in total lifetime revenue.
The consumer app closes April 26, 2026, with the API following in September.
Founded by former Apple executives Imran Chaudhri and Bethany Bongiorno, backed by $230 million from investors including OpenAI CEO Sam Altman and Salesforce CEO Marc Benioff, Humane promised a “post-smartphone” future.
The AI Pin's TED Talk captivated audiences.
The R1's CES debut sold 100,000 units.
Humane's $699 device with a $24/month subscription required selling 100,000+ units just to approach viability — they shipped fewer than 10,000.
The cluster of failures in 2025-2026 does not signal that AI products are nonviable."},{"url":"https://www.siliconrepublic.com/business/humane-ai-pin-product-returns-outpace-sales","title":"The Humane AI Pin is facing a wave of product returns","content":"This data suggests that the Humane AI Pin managed to gain more than $9m in lifetime sales, but that around 1,000 purchases were cancelled before shipping.
A source told The Verge that the number of sold units still with customers is falling closer to 7,000, compared to around 8,000 units in June.
The New York Times reported in June that HP is considering purchasing the company, and The Information recently reported that Humane is trying to raise more funding."},{"url":"https://www.linkedin.com/news/story/humane-ai-pin-returns-outrun-sales-6870634/","title":"Humane AI Pin returns outrun sales | LinkedIn","content":"Humane is seeing returns outpace purchases of its artificial intelligence Pin, The Verge reports, citing internal sales data.
Since releasing the $699 AI Pin in April, Humane has shipped 10,000 pins to date — well below its first-year target of 100,000 units.

Humane, the AI hardware startup behind the AI Pin, has been acquired by HP for $116 million. The company is immediately discontinuing sales of its $499 AI Pin, and existing devices will stop functioning entirely on February 28, 2025, at 12 PM PST. After that date, Humane’s AI Pins will no longer connect to servers or support calling, messaging, AI queries, or cloud access. HP’s acquisition includes Humane’s engineers, product managers, and select AI technologies, such as its CosmOS AI operating system, which could be integrated into HP’s personal computers, printers, and AI-powered ecosystems.
